title: The EfficientNet Family
concept: EfficientNet models such as B0, B1, B2, and beyond are best understood as scaled versions of one base design, showing what happens when a shared architecture is grown in a systematic way rather than reinvented from scratch each time.

exercises.prompt: Recap: in EfficientNet, why can scaling only one axis be wasteful?

options.text: Because another axis may stay the bottleneck and limit the benefit of the extra compute
Because CNNs are only allowed to scale once
Because bottlenecks exist only in hardware, never in model design

exercises.explain: Exactly — now we can see how EfficientNet turns that idea into a whole family of related models.

cards.title: A family, not five unrelated strangers
cards.body: When you see names like EfficientNet-B0, B1, and B2, it is easy to imagine completely different architectures. But the key idea is simpler: they are related members of one family, where a base design is scaled up in a planned way.

cards.title: What do B0, B1, B2... mean?
cards.body: EfficientNet uses a base model called B0. Larger variants such as B1, B2, and beyond keep the same overall design philosophy while increasing scale. The names mostly signal 'more scaled versions of the same core idea,' not 'totally new inventions each time.'

cards.title: Same blueprint, different building sizes
cards.body: Think of one architectural blueprint used to build a small store, a medium store, and a larger store. The layout concept stays familiar, but dimensions grow to fit different budgets and traffic levels. EfficientNet variants work like that more than like separate species.

cards.title: Pick the family member for the job
cards.body: A lightweight device might use a smaller EfficientNet variant because latency and memory matter most. A server with more budget might use a larger variant for extra accuracy. The choice is often about picking the right point along one scaling family rather than switching to a completely different design philosophy.

cards.title: Bigger number does not mean different idea
cards.body: It is easy to read model names like software version numbers and assume each one is a brand-new architecture. For EfficientNet, the more important mental model is continuity: later family members mainly show how the same balanced-scaling idea behaves at larger sizes.

cards.title: How to read the family
cards.body: The model names are best seen as steps along one ladder.

left.text:
B0 starts the family
Defines the core architecture
Acts as the reference point

right.text:
B1, B2, and beyond
Grow the same design carefully
Offer larger-capacity options

exercises.prompt: What is the best way to think about EfficientNet-B2 compared with EfficientNet-B0?

options.text: It is a larger scaled member of the same design family
It abandons EfficientNet and becomes a transformer
It is a random architecture with a similar name
It only changes the training labels, not the model

exercises.explain: Right — EfficientNet variants are mainly scaled relatives, not disconnected ideas.

exercises.prompt: True or false: EfficientNet family names mainly help you think about choosing different scale points from one shared architecture idea.
exercises.explain: True — the family perspective is the key lesson here.

exercises.sentence: EfficientNet-B0 is the ___ model that later EfficientNet variants scale from.
exercises.explain: Exactly — B0 is the starting blueprint for the family.

exercises.prompt: Which product decision matches the EfficientNet family idea?

options.text: Use a smaller family member on edge hardware and a larger family member on servers, while keeping the same basic architectural philosophy
Treat every EfficientNet name as a totally unrelated architecture and ignore the shared scaling logic
Assume B0 is only for training and can never be deployed

exercises.explain: Yes — the family structure helps you map one architecture idea to multiple deployment budgets.

exercises.prompt: A company wants one vision architecture for phones, laptops, and cloud servers. Why is the EfficientNet family concept useful for that goal?

options.text: Because one base design can be scaled into multiple related variants, letting different devices choose different family members without changing the core idea
Because every device should run the exact same model size no matter the budget
Because EfficientNet variants are all identical in compute and memory
Because model families matter only for naming, not deployment choices

exercises.explain: Exactly. EfficientNet showed that one carefully chosen base architecture can serve many budgets through systematic scaling. Next, we will step back and ask what this taught model designers more broadly.
